title: Mantra Japa (Repetition)
summary: The practice of repeating a mantra, typically on a 108-bead mala. A core daily practice in every Hindu sampradaya.

steps.title: Choose your mantra
steps.instruction: Beginners commonly use: Om Namah Shivaya (Shaiva), Om Namo Narayanaya or Hare Krishna mahamantra (Vaishnava), Om Aim Hrim Klim Chamundaye Viccha (Shakta), Om Gam Ganapataye Namaha (Ganesha), Gayatri (any Smarta practice). If you have a diksha mantra from a guru, use it; otherwise the generic ishta-devata mantra is appropriate.

steps.title: Get a mala
steps.instruction: 108 beads plus a meru (summit) bead. Tulasi mala for Vaishnavas; rudraksha for Shaivas; sphatika (crystal) or red sandalwood is universal.

steps.title: Posture and time
steps.instruction: Sit with a straight spine, facing east or north. Brahma-muhurta (roughly 4:00–5:30 AM) is traditional but any consistent quiet time is fine.

steps.title: Hold the mala correctly
steps.instruction: Hold the mala in the right hand, draped over the middle finger. Use the thumb to pull each bead past. Do not cross the meru bead — when you reach it after 108, reverse direction for the next round.

steps.title: Start with a fixed count
steps.instruction: A typical daily sadhana is one mala (108 repetitions), scaling up over time. Some traditions prescribe 3, 11, or 16 rounds. Consistency daily is more important than long occasional sessions.

steps.title: Close with dedication
steps.instruction: End with 'sarvam krishnarpanam astu' (Vaishnava) or a similar line dedicating the fruit of the practice to the deity.

sources.quote: Among sacrifices, I am the japa-yajna...
